    20â†’# Betrothed numbers
    21â†’

    23â†’- [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betrothed_numbers)
    24â†’- [OEIS A005276](https://oeis.org/A005276)

    27â†’namespace BetrothedNumbers
    28â†’
    29â†’open scoped ArithmeticFunction.sigma
    30â†’
    31â†’/--
    32â†’Two natural numbers $m$ and $n$ are **betrothed**  (or quasi-amicable) if $\sigma(m) = \sigma(n) = m + n + 1$,
    33â†’where $\sigma$ is the sum-of-divisors function. Equivalently, the sum of the proper divisors
    34â†’of $m$ equals $n + 1$, and the sum of the proper divisors of $n$ equals $m + 1$.
    35â†’-/
    36â†’@[mk_iff]
    37â†’structure IsBetrothed (m n : â„•) : Prop where
    38â†’  left : دƒ 1 m = m + n + 1
    39â†’  right : دƒ 1 n = m + n + 1
    40â†’
    41â†’/-- The smallest known betrothed pair $(48, 75)$. -/
    42â†’@[category test, AMS 11]

    46â†’/-- `IsBetrothed` is symmetric. -/

    53â†’**Same parity betrothed numbers conjecture.**

    55â†’(both even or both odd)?

    57â†’All known betrothed pairs consist of one even and one odd number.

    63â†’  sorry
    64â†’
    65â†’/--
    66â†’**Infinitude of betrothed numbers conjecture.**
    67â†’Are there infinitely many betrothed number pairs?
    68â†’
    69â†’-/
    70â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]

    72â†’    answer(sorry) â†” {p : â„• أ— â„• | p.1 < p.2 âˆ§ IsBetrothed p.1 p.2}.Infinite := by
    73â†’  sorry
    74â†’
    75â†’end BetrothedNumbers

    35â†’proper divisors of $n$, but this is not true for any $m âˆ£ n$ with $m < n$. Does:

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 470
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/470](https://www.erdosproblems.com/470)
    23â†’-/
    24â†’
    25â†’namespace Erdos470
    26â†’
    27â†’/--
    28â†’Primitive weird numbers are weird numbers such that no proper divisor of $n$ are weird.
    29â†’-/
    30â†’def PrimitiveWeird (n : â„•) := n.Weird âˆ§ âˆ€ d âˆˆ n.properDivisors, آ¬d.Weird
    31â†’
    32â†’/--
    33â†’The abundancy index is the sum of the divisors of $n$ divided by $n$.
    34â†’-/
    35â†’def AbundancyIndex (n : â„•) : â„ڑ := (âˆ‘ d âˆˆ n.divisors, d) / n
    36â†’
    37â†’/--
    38â†’Are there any odd weird numbers?
    39â†’-/
    40â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]
    41â†’theorem erdos_470.part1 : answer(sorry) â†” âˆƒ n : â„•, n.Weird âˆ§ Odd n := by
    42â†’  sorry
    43â†’
    44â†’/--
    45â†’Are there infinitely many primitive weird numbers?
    46â†’-/
    47â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]
    48â†’theorem erdos_470.part2 : answer(sorry) â†” Set.Infinite PrimitiveWeird := by
    49â†’  sorry
    50â†’
    51â†’/--
    52â†’Benkoski and Erdإ‘s [BeEr74](https://mathscinet.ams.org/mathscinet/relay-station?mr=347726) proved
    53â†’that the set of weird numbers has positive density.
    54â†’-/
    55â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    56â†’theorem erdos_470.variants.weird_pos_density : {n : â„• | n.Weird}.HasPosDensity := by
    57â†’  sorry
    58â†’
    59â†’/--
    60â†’The smallest weird number is 70.
    61â†’-/
    62â†’@[category high_school, AMS 11]
    63â†’theorem erdos_470.variants.smallest_weird_eq_70 : (âˆ€ n < 70, آ¬n.Weird) âˆ§ (70).Weird := by
    64â†’  sorry
    65â†’
    66â†’/--
    67â†’Melfi [Me15](https://mathscinet.ams.org/mathscinet/relay-station?mr=3276337) has proved that there
    68â†’are infinitely many primitive weird numbers, conditional on the fact that
    69â†’$p_{n+1} - p_n < \frac{1}{10} \sqrt{p_n}$ for all large $n$, which in turn would follow from
    70â†’well-known conjectures concerning prime gaps.

    72â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    73â†’theorem erdos_470.variants.prime_gap_imp_inf_prim_weird :
    74â†’    âˆ€ل¶  n in Filter.atTop, primeGap n < âˆڑ (n.nth Nat.Prime) / 10 â†’

    81â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    82â†’theorem erdos_470.variants.odd_weird_10_pow_21 : âˆ€ n < 10 ^ 21, Odd n â†’ آ¬n.Weird := by

    84â†’
    85â†’/--
    86â†’Liddy and Riedl [LiRi18](https://ideaexchange.uakron.edu/honors_research_projects/728/) have shown
    87â†’that an odd weird number must have at least 6 prime divisors.
    88â†’-/
    89â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    90â†’theorem erdos_470.variants.odd_weird_prime_div :
    91â†’    âˆ€ n : â„•, Odd n â†’ n.Weird â†’ 6 â‰¤ {m | m âˆˆ n.divisors âˆ§ m.Prime}.ncard := by
    92â†’  sorry
    93â†’
    94â†’/--
    95â†’If there are no odd weird numbers then every weird number has abundancy index < 4.
    96â†’-/
    97â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    98â†’theorem erdos_470.variants.abundancy_index :
    99â†’    (âˆ€ n : â„•, n.Weird â†’ آ¬Odd n) â†’ âˆ€ n, n.Weird â†’ AbundancyIndex n < 4 := by
   100â†’  sorry

    19â†’/-!
    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 316
    21â†’
    22â†’*References:*
    23â†’- [erdosproblems.com/316](https://www.erdosproblems.com/316)
    24â†’- [Sa97] Sأ،ndor, Csaba, On a problem of Erdإ‘s. J. Number Theory (1997), 203-210.
    25â†’-/
    26â†’
    27â†’namespace Erdos316
    28â†’
    29â†’/--
    30â†’Is it true that if $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}\setminus\{1\}$ is a finite set with
    31â†’$\sum_{n \in A} \frac{1}{n} < 2$ then there is a partition $A=A_1 \sqcup A_2$
    32â†’such that $\sum_{n \in A_i} \frac{1}{n} < 1$ for $i=1,2$?
    33â†’
    34â†’This is not true in general, as shown by Sأ،ndor [Sa97].
    35â†’
    36â†’The minimal counterexample is $\{2,3,4,5,6,7,10,11,13,14,15\}$, found by Tom Stobart.
    37â†’
    38â†’This was formalized in Lean by Mehta.
    39â†’-/
    40â†’@[category research formally solved using lean4 at
    41â†’"https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/blob/main/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/316.lean",
    42â†’AMS 5 11]
    43â†’theorem erdos_316 : answer(False) â†” âˆ€ A : Finset â„•, 0 âˆ‰ A â†’ 1 âˆ‰ A â†’
    44â†’    âˆ‘ n âˆˆ A, (1 / n : â„ڑ) < 2 â†’ âˆƒ (Aâ‚پ Aâ‚‚ : Finset â„•),
    45â†’      Disjoint Aâ‚پ Aâ‚‚ âˆ§ A = Aâ‚پ âˆھ Aâ‚‚ âˆ§
    46â†’      âˆ‘ n âˆˆ Aâ‚پ, (1 / n : â„ڑ) < 1 âˆ§ âˆ‘ n âˆˆ Aâ‚‚, (1 / n : â„ڑ) <  1 := by
    47â†’  simp only [one_div, false_iff, not_forall, not_exists, not_and, not_lt]
    48â†’  let A : Finset â„• := {2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 10, 11, 13, 14, 15}
    49â†’  refine âں¨A, by decide, by decide, by decide +kernel, ?_âں©
    50â†’  suffices h : âˆ€ B âٹ† A, âˆ‘ n âˆˆ B, (n : â„ڑ)âپ»آ¹ < 1 â†’ 1 â‰¤ âˆ‘ n âˆˆ A \ B, (n : â„ڑ)âپ»آ¹ by
    51â†’    rintro B C hBC hA hlt
    52â†’    have : C = A \ B := by rw [hA, Finset.union_sdiff_cancel_left hBC]
    53â†’    exact this â–¸ h B (by simp [hA]) hlt
    54â†’  decide +kernel
    55â†’
    56â†’/-- This is not true if $A$ is a multiset, for example $2,3,3,5,5,5,5$. -/
    57â†’@[category high_school, AMS 5 11]
    58â†’lemma erdos_316.variants.multiset : âˆƒ A : Multiset â„•, 0 âˆ‰ A âˆ§ 1 âˆ‰ A âˆ§
    59â†’    (A.map ((1 : â„ڑ) / آ·)).sum < 2 âˆ§ âˆ€ (Aâ‚پ Aâ‚‚ : Multiset â„•),
    60â†’      A = Aâ‚پ + Aâ‚‚ â†’
    61â†’        1 â‰¤ (Aâ‚پ.map ((1 : â„ڑ) / آ·)).sum âˆ¨ 1 â‰¤ (Aâ‚‚.map ((1 : â„ڑ) / آ·)).sum := by
    62â†’  let A : Multiset â„• := {2, 3, 3, 5, 5, 5, 5}
    63â†’  refine âں¨A, by decide, by decide, by decide +kernel, ?_âں©
    64â†’  suffices h : âˆ€ B âˆˆ A.powerset, 1 â‰¤ (B.map (fun x â†¦ (x : â„ڑ)âپ»آ¹)).sum âˆ¨
    65â†’      1 â‰¤ ((A - B).map (fun x â†¦ (x : â„ڑ)âپ»آ¹)).sum by
    66â†’    intro B C hBC
    67â†’    have : C = A - B := by simp [hBC]
    68â†’    simp only [Multiset.pure_def, Multiset.bind_def, Multiset.bind_singleton, Multiset.map_map,
    69â†’      Function.comp_apply, one_div] at h âٹ¢
    70â†’    exact this â–¸ h B (by simp [hBC])
    71â†’  decide +kernel
    72â†’
    73â†’/--
    74â†’This is not true in general, as shown by Sأ،ndor [Sa97], who observed that the proper divisors of
    75â†’$120$ form a counterexample. More generally, Sأ،ndor shows that for any $n\geq 2$ there exists a
    76â†’finite set $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}\backslash\{1\}$ with $\sum_{k\in A}\frac{1}{k} < n$ and no
    77â†’partition into $n$ parts each of which has $\sum_{k\in A_i}\frac{1}{k}<1$.
    78â†’-/
    79â†’@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    80â†’theorem erdos_316.variants.generalized (n : â„•) (hn : 2 â‰¤ n) : âˆƒ A : Finset â„•,
    81â†’    A.Nonempty âˆ§ 0 âˆ‰ A âˆ§ 1 âˆ‰ A âˆ§ âˆ‘ k âˆˆ A, (1 / k : â„ڑ) < n âˆ§ âˆ€ P : Finpartition A,
    82â†’    P.parts.card = n â†’ âˆƒ p âˆˆ P.parts, 1 â‰¤ âˆ‘ n âˆˆ p, (1 / n : â„ڑ) := by
    83â†’  sorry
    84â†’
    85â†’end Erdos316
    86â†’

    19â†’/-!
    20â†’# Leinster Groups
    21â†’
    22â†’A finite group is a Leinster group if the sum of the orders of all its normal subgroups
    23â†’equals twice the group's order.
    24â†’
    25â†’*References:*
    26â†’* [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leinster_group)
    27â†’* Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups".
    28â†’  [arXiv:math/0104012](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0104012)
    29â†’
    30â†’TODO: The following properties from the Wikipedia article can also be formalized:
    31â†’- There are no Leinster groups that are symmetric or alternating.
    32â†’- There is no Leinster group of order pآ²qآ² where p, q are primes.
    33â†’- No finite semi-simple group is Leinster.
    34â†’- No p-group can be a Leinster group.
    35â†’- All abelian Leinster groups are cyclic with order equal to a perfect number.
    36â†’-/
    37â†’
    38â†’namespace LeinsterGroup
    39â†’
    40â†’open scoped Classical
    41â†’
    42â†’/--
    43â†’A finite group `G` is a **Leinster group** if the sum of the orders of all its normal subgroups
    44â†’equals twice the group's order.
    45â†’-/
    46â†’def IsLeinster (G : Type*) [Group G] [Fintype G] : Prop :=
    47â†’  âˆ‘ H : {H : Subgroup G // H.Normal}, Nat.card H = 2 * Fintype.card G
    48â†’
    49â†’/--
    50â†’**Conjecture:** Are there infinitely many Leinster groups?
    51â†’
    52â†’This asks whether there exist infinitely many (non-isomorphic) finite groups that are
    53â†’Leinster groups.
    54â†’
    55â†’Formalized via the negation of "Does there exist an n such that all Leinster groups have
    56â†’order less than n".
    57â†’-/
    58â†’@[category research open, AMS 20]
    59â†’theorem infinitely_many_leinster_groups : answer(sorry) â†”
    60â†’    آ¬âˆƒ n : â„•, âˆ€ G : Type, âˆ€ (_ : Group G) (_ : Fintype G),
    61â†’      IsLeinster G â†’ Fintype.card G < n := by
    62â†’  sorry
    63â†’
    64â†’/--
    65â†’Cyclic groups of perfect number order are Leinster groups.
    66â†’
    67â†’This follows from the fact that for a cyclic group, all subgroups are normal and correspond
    68â†’to divisors of the group order, and a number is perfect if and only if the sum of its divisors
    69â†’(including itself) equals twice the number.
    70â†’-/
    71â†’@[category API, AMS 20]
    72â†’theorem cyclic_of_perfect_is_leinster (G : Type*) [Group G] [Fintype G]
    73â†’    [IsCyclic G] (h_perfect : Nat.Perfect (Fintype.card G)) :
    74â†’    IsLeinster G := by
    75â†’  sorry
    76â†’
    77â†’/--
    78â†’An abelian group is a Leinster group if and only if it is cyclic with order equal
    79â†’to a perfect number.
    80â†’
    81â†’Reference: Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups". Theorem 2.1.
    82â†’-/
    83â†’@[category research solved, AMS 20]
    84â†’theorem abelian_is_leinster_iff_cyclic_perfect (G : Type*) [CommGroup G] [Fintype G] :
    85â†’    IsLeinster G â†” IsCyclic G âˆ§ Nat.Perfect (Fintype.card G) := by
    86â†’  sorry
    87â†’
    88â†’/--
    89â†’Non-abelian Leinster groups exist.
    90â†’
    91â†’For example, `Sâ‚ƒ أ— Câ‚…` (order 30) and `Aâ‚… أ— Câ‚پâ‚…â‚پâ‚‚â‚ˆ` are Leinster groups.
    92â†’
    93â†’Reference: Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups".
    94â†’-/
    95â†’@[category research solved, AMS 20]
    96â†’theorem exists_nonabelian_leinster_group :
    97â†’    âˆƒ G : Type, âˆƒ (_ : Group G) (_ : Fintype G),
    98â†’      IsLeinster G âˆ§ آ¬ âˆ€ (a b : G), a * b = b * a := by
    99â†’  sorry
   100â†’
   101â†’/--
   102â†’The dihedral group `DihedralGroup n` (of order `2n`) is a Leinster group if and only if `n` is
   103â†’an odd perfect number. This gives a one-to-one correspondence between dihedral Leinster groups
   104â†’and odd perfect numbers.
   105â†’
   106â†’In particular, the existence of odd perfect numbers is equivalent to the existence of
   107â†’dihedral Leinster groups.
   108â†’
   109â†’Reference: Leinster, Tom (2001). "Perfect numbers and groups".
   110â†’-/
   111â†’@[category research solved, AMS 20]
   112â†’theorem dihedral_is_leinster_iff_odd_perfect (n : â„•) [NeZero n] :
   113â†’    IsLeinster (DihedralGroup n) â†” Nat.Perfect n âˆ§ Odd n := by
   114â†’  sorry
   115â†’
   116â†’end LeinsterGroup
   117â†’

    17â†’import Mathlib.Algebra.Order.Ring.Nat
    18â†’import Mathlib.Algebra.Order.Star.Basic
    19â†’import Mathlib.Data.Nat.PrimeFin
    20â†’
    21â†’namespace Nat
    22â†’
    23â†’/--
    24â†’A natural number $n$ is said to be $k$-full (or $k$-powerful) if for every prime factor $p$ of $n$,
    25â†’the $k$-th power $p^k$ also divides $n$.
    26â†’-/
    27â†’def Full (k : â„•) (n : â„•) : Prop := âˆ€ p âˆˆ n.primeFactors, p^k âˆ£ n
    28â†’
    29â†’instance Full.decide : âˆ€ k n, Decidable (Full k n) := by
    30â†’  intro k n
    31â†’  dsimp [Full]
    32â†’  infer_instance
    33â†’
    34â†’/-- Every natural number is $0$-full. -/
    35â†’theorem Full.zero_left (n : â„•) : (0 : â„•).Full n := by
    36â†’  simp [Nat.Full]
    37â†’
    38â†’/-- Every natural number is $1$-full. -/
    39â†’theorem Full.one_left (n : â„•) : (1 : â„•).Full n := by
    40â†’  aesop (add simp [Nat.Full])
    41â†’
    42â†’/-- $0$ is always $k$-full. -/
    43â†’theorem Full.zero_right (k : â„•) : k.Full 0 := by
    44â†’  simp [Nat.Full]
    45â†’
    46â†’/-- $1$ is always $k$-full. -/
    47â†’theorem Full.one_right (k : â„•) : k.Full 1 := by
    48â†’  simp [Nat.Full]
    49â†’
    50â†’/--
    51â†’A [Powerful number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Powerful_number) is a natural number $n$ where
    52â†’for every prime divisor $p$, $p^2$ divides $n$.
    53â†’Powerful numbers are also known as "squareful", "square-full", or "$2$-full".
    54â†’-/
    55â†’abbrev Powerful : â„• â†’ Prop := (2).Full
    56â†’
    57â†’instance Powerful.decide : âˆ€ n, Decidable (Powerful n) := by
    58â†’  intro n
    59â†’  dsimp [Powerful, Full]
    60â†’  apply Finset.decidableDforallFinset
    61â†’
    62â†’theorem full_of_le_full (k : â„•) (n : â„•) {m : â„•} (hk : k â‰¤ m) (h : m.Full n) : k.Full n :=
    63â†’  fun p a â†¦ pow_dvd_of_le_of_pow_dvd hk (h p a)
    64â†’
    65â†’/-- If $n \equiv p \pmod{p ^ (k + 1)}$, for a prime $p$ then $n$ is not $(k + 1)$-full. -/
    66â†’theorem not_full_of_prime_mod_prime_sq (n : â„•) (k : â„•) {p : â„•} (hp : p.Prime)
    67â†’    (h : n % p ^ (k + 1) = p) : آ¬ (k + 1).Full n := by
    68â†’  rw [Full]
    69â†’  push_neg
    70â†’  use p
    71â†’  simp [mem_primeFactors, hp, ne_eq, true_and]
    72â†’  constructor
    73â†’  آ· rw [â†گNat.div_add_mod n (p ^ (k + 1)), h]
    74â†’    have : p âˆ£ p ^ (k + 1) := by exact Dvd.intro_left (p.pow k) rfl
    75â†’    simp
    76â†’    exact âں¨Dvd.dvd.mul_right this (n / p ^ (k + 1)), fun a â†¦ Prime.ne_zero hpâں©
    77â†’  آ· intro h
    78â†’    simp_all [Nat.dvd_iff_mod_eq_zero.mp h]
    79â†’    aesop
    80â†’
    81â†’
    82â†’open Lean Meta Qq in
    83â†’/-- Simproc to compute the set `Nat.primeFactors`. -/
    84â†’dsimproc primeFactorsEq (Nat.primeFactors _) := fun e â†¦ do
    85â†’  unless e.isAppOfArity `Nat.primeFactors 1 do return .continue
    86â†’  let some n â†گ fromExpr? e.appArg! | return .continue
    87â†’  let outAsList : List Q(â„•) := (unsafe n.primeFactors.val.unquot).map mkNatLit
    88â†’  let outAsFinset : Q(Finset â„•) := outAsList.foldl (fun s n â†¦ q(insert $n $s)) q({})
    89â†’  return .done outAsFinset

    17â†’import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Squarefree

    19â†’/-!
    20â†’# Primitive elements of a set with respect to divisibility
    21â†’
    22â†’An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S âٹ† â„•` if `n âˆˆ S` and no proper divisor of `n` is in `S`.
    23â†’
    24â†’This concept appears naturally when studying sequences closed under "divisor inheritance" -
    25â†’if every member `n` of a sequence has the property that `m * n` is also in the sequence
    26â†’for coprime `m`, then the primitive elements generate the entire sequence.
    27â†’
    28â†’## Main definitions
    29â†’
    30â†’* `Set.IsPrimitive`: A natural number `n` is primitive in `S` if `n âˆˆ S` and
    31â†’  `n.properDivisors` is disjoint from `S`.
    32â†’* `Set.primitives`: The set of primitive elements of `S`.
    33â†’
    34â†’## Main results
    35â†’
    36â†’* `Set.IsPrimitive.mem`: A primitive element is in the set.
    37â†’* `Set.IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor`: Proper divisors of a primitive element are not
    38â†’  in the set.
    39â†’* `Set.one_isPrimitive_iff`: `1` is primitive in `S` iff `1 âˆˆ S`.
    40â†’* `Set.prime_isPrimitive_iff`: A prime `p` is primitive in `S` iff `p âˆˆ S` and `1 âˆ‰ S`.
    41â†’-/
    42â†’
    43â†’namespace Set
    44â†’
    45â†’/-- An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S âٹ† â„•` if `n âˆˆ S` and no proper divisor of `n`
    46â†’is in `S`. -/
    47â†’def IsPrimitive (S : Set â„•) (n : â„•) : Prop :=
    48â†’  n âˆˆ S âˆ§ Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set â„•) S
    49â†’
    50â†’/-- The set of primitive elements of `S`. -/
    51â†’def primitives (S : Set â„•) : Set â„• :=
    52â†’  {n | S.IsPrimitive n}
    53â†’
    54â†’theorem IsPrimitive.mem {S : Set â„•} {n : â„•} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) : n âˆˆ S := h.1
    55â†’
    56â†’theorem IsPrimitive.disjoint_properDivisors {S : Set â„•} {n : â„•} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) :
    57â†’    Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set â„•) S := h.2
    58â†’
    59â†’theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor {S : Set â„•} {n d : â„•} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    60â†’    (hd : d âˆˆ n.properDivisors) : d âˆ‰ S :=
    61â†’  Set.disjoint_left.mp h.2 hd
    62â†’
    63â†’theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_dvd_of_lt {S : Set â„•} {n d : â„•} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    64â†’    (hdvd : d âˆ£ n) (hlt : d < n) : d âˆ‰ S :=
    65â†’  h.not_mem_of_properDivisor (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr âں¨hdvd, hltâں©)
    66â†’
    67â†’theorem isPrimitive_iff {S : Set â„•} {n : â„•} :
    68â†’    S.IsPrimitive n â†” n âˆˆ S âˆ§ âˆ€ d âˆˆ n.properDivisors, d âˆ‰ S := by
    69â†’  simp only [IsPrimitive, Set.disjoint_left, Finset.mem_coe]
    70â†’
    71â†’theorem isPrimitive_iff' {S : Set â„•} {n : â„•} (_hn : 0 < n) :
    72â†’    S.IsPrimitive n â†” n âˆˆ S âˆ§ âˆ€ d, d âˆ£ n â†’ d < n â†’ d âˆ‰ S := by
    73â†’  rw [isPrimitive_iff]
    74â†’  constructor <;> intro âں¨hmem, hdivâں©
    75â†’  آ· exact âں¨hmem, fun d hd hlt => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr âں¨hd, hltâں©)âں©
    76â†’  آ· exact âں¨hmem, fun d hd => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).1 (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).2âں©
    77â†’
    78â†’theorem mem_primitives_iff {S : Set â„•} {n : â„•} : n âˆˆ S.primitives â†” S.IsPrimitive n := Iff.rfl
    79â†’
    80â†’@[simp]
    81â†’theorem primitives_subset {S : Set â„•} : S.primitives âٹ† S := fun _ h => h.mem
    82â†’
    83â†’theorem one_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set â„•} : S.IsPrimitive 1 â†” 1 âˆˆ S := by
    84â†’  simp [isPrimitive_iff, Nat.properDivisors_one]
    85â†’
    86â†’theorem prime_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set â„•} {p : â„•} (hp : p.Prime) :
    87â†’    S.IsPrimitive p â†” p âˆˆ S âˆ§ 1 âˆ‰ S := by
    88â†’  simp [isPrimitive_iff, hp.properDivisors]
    89â†’
    90â†’end Set

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 418
    21â†’
    22â†’*References:*
    23â†’- [erdosproblems.com/418](https://www.erdosproblems.com/418)
    24â†’- [BaLu05] Banks, William D. and Luca, Florian, Nonaliquots and {R}obbins numbers. Colloq. Math.
    25â†’  (2005), 27--32.
    26â†’- [BrSc95] Browkin, J. and Schinzel, A., On integers not of the form {$n-\phi(n)$}. Colloq. Math.
    27â†’  (1995), 55-58.
    28â†’- [ChZh11] Chen, Yong-Gao and Zhao, Qing-Qing, Nonaliquot numbers. Publ. Math. Debrecen (2011),
    29â†’  439--442.
    30â†’- [Er73b] Erdإ‘s, P., \"أœber die Zahlen der Form $\sigma (n)-n$ und $n-\phi(n)$. Elem. Math.
    31â†’  (1973), 83-86.
    32â†’- [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.
    33â†’- [PoPo16] Pollack, Paul and Pomerance, Carl, Some problems of Erdإ‘s on the sum-of-divisors
    34â†’  function. Trans. Amer. Math. Soc. Ser. B (2016), 1-26.

    37â†’open scoped ArithmeticFunction.sigma

    39â†’namespace Erdos418
    40â†’
    41â†’/--
    42â†’Are there infinitely many integers not of the form $n - \phi(n)$?
    43â†’
    44â†’Asked by Erdإ‘s and Sierpiإ„ski. Numbers not of the form we call non-cototients.
    45â†’
    46â†’Browkin and Schinzel [BrSc95] provided an affirmative answer to this question, proving that any
    47â†’integer of the shape $2^{k}\cdot 509203$ for $k\geq 1$ is a non-cototient.
    48â†’
    49â†’This is discussed in problem B36 of Guy's collection [Gu04].
    50â†’
    51â†’This was formalized in Lean by Alexeev using Aristotle.
    52â†’-/
    53â†’@[category research formally solved using lean4 at
    54â†’"https://github.com/plby/lean-proofs/blob/main/src/v4.24.0/ErdosProblems/Erdos418.lean", AMS 11]
    55â†’theorem erdos_418 : answer(True) â†” { (n - n.totient : â„•) | n }ل¶œ.Infinite := by
    56â†’  sorry
    57â†’
    58â†’/--
    59â†’It follows from a slight strengthening of the Goldbach conjecture that every odd number can be
    60â†’written as $n - \phi(n)$.
    61â†’In particular, we assume that every even number greater than 6 can be written as the sum of two
    62â†’*distinct* primes, in contrast to the usual Goldbach conjecture that every even number greater than
    63â†’2 can be written as the sum of two primes.
    64â†’-/
    65â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    66â†’theorem erdos_418.variants.conditional
    67â†’    (goldbach : âˆ€ (n : â„•), 6 < n â†’ Even n â†’ âˆƒ p q, p â‰  q âˆ§ p.Prime âˆ§ q.Prime âˆ§ n = p + q)
    68â†’    (m : â„•) (h : Odd m) :
    69â†’    âˆƒ n, m + n.totient = n := by
    70â†’  obtain rfl | rfl | rfl | h7m : m = 1 âˆ¨ m = 3 âˆ¨ m = 5 âˆ¨ 7 â‰¤ m := by
    71â†’    obtain âں¨m, rflâں© := h
    72â†’    omega
    73â†’  آ· exact âں¨2, rflâں©
    74â†’  آ· exact âں¨9, rflâں©
    75â†’  آ· exact âں¨25, rflâں©
    76â†’  obtain âں¨p, q, hpq, hp, hq, hmâں© := goldbach (m + 1) (by omega) (by simpa [parity_simps])
    77â†’  use p * q
    78â†’  have h2p : 2 â‰¤ p := hp.two_le
    79â†’  have h2q : 2 â‰¤ q := hq.two_le
    80â†’  rw [Nat.totient_mul, Nat.totient_prime hp, Nat.totient_prime hq]
    81â†’  آ· obtain âں¨p, rflâں© := le_iff_exists_add'.1 h2p
    82â†’    obtain âں¨q, rflâں© := le_iff_exists_add'.1 h2q
    83â†’    simp only [Nat.add_one_sub_one]
    84â†’    linear_combination hm
    85â†’  rwa [Nat.coprime_primes hp hq]
    86â†’
    87â†’/--
    88â†’Erdإ‘s [Er73b] has shown that a positive density set of natural numbers cannot be written as
    89â†’$\sigma(n)-n$ (numbers not of this form are called nonaliquot, or sometimes untouchable).
    90â†’-/
    91â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    92â†’theorem erdos_418.variants.sigma :
    93â†’    âˆƒ (S : Set â„•) (hS : S.HasPosDensity),
    94â†’      S âٹ† { (دƒ 1 n - n : â„•) | n }ل¶œ := by
    95â†’  sorry
    96â†’
    97â†’/--
    98â†’A solution to erdos_418 was shown by Browkin and Schinzel [BrSc95] by showing that any integer of
    99â†’the form $2^(k + 1)\cdot 509203$ is not of the form $n - \phi(n)$.
   100â†’-/
   101â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   102â†’theorem erdos_418.variants.soln :
   103â†’    { 2 ^ (k + 1) * 509203 | k } âٹ† { (n - n.totient : â„•) | n }ل¶œ := by
   104â†’  sorry
   105â†’
   106â†’/--
   107â†’It is open whether the set of non-cototients has positive density.
   108â†’-/
   109â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]
   110â†’theorem erdos_418.variants.density :
   111â†’    answer(sorry) â†” âˆƒ (S : Set â„•) (hS : S.HasPosDensity), S âٹ† { (n - n.totient : â„•) | n }ل¶œ := by
   112â†’  sorry

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 419
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/419](https://www.erdosproblems.com/419)
    23â†’
    24â†’If $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$, then what is the set of limit
    25â†’points of $\tau((n+1)!) / \tau(n!)$?
    26â†’
    27â†’Erdإ‘s and Graham [ErGr80] noted that any number of the shape $1 + 1/k$ for $k \geq 1$
    28â†’is a limit point (and thus so too is $1$), but knew of no others.
    29â†’
    30â†’Sawhney (and independently Erdإ‘sâ€“Grahamâ€“Iviؤ‡â€“Pomerance [EGIP96]) proved that
    31â†’these are the only limit points: the set of limit points of the ratio is
    32â†’exactly $\{1 + 1/k : k \geq 1\} \cup \{1\}$.
    33â†’
    34â†’[ErGr80] Erdإ‘s, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial
    35â†’number theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathأ©matique (1980).
    36â†’
    37â†’[EGIP96] Erdإ‘s, P., Graham, R., Iviؤ‡, A., and Pomerance, C., _On the number of
    38â†’divisors of n!_. Analytic Number Theory (Proceedings of a Conference in Honor of
    39â†’Heini Halberstam) (1996).
    40â†’-/

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 420
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/420](https://www.erdosproblems.com/420)
    23â†’
    24â†’If $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$, define
    25â†’$$F(f, n) = \frac{\tau((n + \lfloor f(n) \rfloor)!)}{\tau(n!)}$$
    26â†’
    27â†’Three questions:
    28â†’1. Is it true that $\lim_{n \to \infty} F((\log n)^C, n) = \infty$ for all sufficiently
    29â†’   large $C$?
    30â†’2. Is it true that $F(\log n, n)$ is everywhere dense in $(1, \infty)$?
    31â†’3. More generally, if $f(n) \leq \log n$ is a monotonic function with $f(n) \to \infty$,
    32â†’   then is $F(f, n)$ everywhere dense in $(1, \infty)$?
    33â†’
    34â†’Erdإ‘s and Graham note it is easy to show $\lim F(n^{1/2}, n) = \infty$.
    35â†’
    36â†’[EGIP96] Erdإ‘s, P., Graham, R., Iviؤ‡, A., and Pomerance, C. proved:
    37â†’- $\liminf F(c \log n, n) = 1$ for any $c > 0$
    38â†’- $\lim F(n^{4/9}, n) = \infty$
    39â†’- if $f(n) = o((\log n)^2)$, then $F(f,n) \sim 1$ for almost all $n$.
    40â†’
    41â†’[ErGr80] Erdإ‘s, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    42â†’theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    43â†’-/
    44â†’
    45â†’open Filter
    46â†’
    47â†’open scoped Topology Real
    48â†’
    49â†’namespace Erdos420
    50â†’
    51â†’/-- The ratio $F(f, n) = \tau((n + \lfloor f(n) \rfloor)!) / \tau(n!)$ where $\tau$ counts
    52â†’divisors. Here $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ and $\lfloor f(n) \rfloor_+$ is the natural
    53â†’number floor of $f(n)$. -/
    54â†’noncomputable def F (f : â„• â†’ â„‌) (n : â„•) : â„‌ :=
    55â†’  ((Nat.divisors (n + âŒٹf nâŒ‹â‚ٹ).factorial).card : â„‌) /
    56â†’  ((Nat.divisors n.factorial).card : â„‌)
    57â†’
    58â†’/--
    59â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 420 (Part 1) [ErGr80, p.83]:
    60â†’
    61â†’Is it true that $\lim_{n \to \infty} F((\log n)^C, n) = \infty$ for all sufficiently large $C$?
    62â†’-/
    63â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]
    64â†’theorem erdos_420 : answer(sorry) â†”
    65â†’    âˆƒ Câ‚€ : â„‌, âˆ€ C : â„‌, C â‰¥ Câ‚€ â†’
    66â†’      Tendsto (fun n : â„• => F (fun m => (Real.log (m : â„‌)) ^ C) n)
    67â†’        atTop atTop := by
    68â†’  sorry
    69â†’
    70â†’/--
    71â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 420 (Part 2) [ErGr80, p.83]:
    72â†’
    73â†’Is it true that $F(\log n, n)$ is everywhere dense in $(1, \infty)$?
    74â†’That is, for any $1 < a < b$, there are infinitely many $n$ with
    75â†’$a < F(\log n, n) < b$.
    76â†’-/
    77â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]
    78â†’theorem erdos_420.variants.dense_log : answer(sorry) â†”
    79â†’    âˆ€ a b : â„‌, 1 < a â†’ a < b â†’
    80â†’      âˆƒل¶  n in atTop,
    81â†’        a < F (fun m => Real.log (m : â„‌)) n âˆ§
    82â†’        F (fun m => Real.log (m : â„‌)) n < b := by
    83â†’  sorry
    84â†’
    85â†’/--
    86â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 420 (Part 3) [ErGr80, p.83]:
    87â†’
    88â†’More generally, if $f(n) \leq \log n$ is a monotonic function such that $f(n) \to \infty$
    89â†’as $n \to \infty$, then is $F(f, n)$ everywhere dense in $(1, \infty)$?
    90â†’-/
    91â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]
    92â†’theorem erdos_420.variants.dense_monotone : answer(sorry) â†”
    93â†’    âˆ€ f : â„• â†’ â„‌, Monotone f â†’
    94â†’      (âˆ€ n : â„•, f n â‰¤ Real.log (n : â„‌)) â†’
    95â†’      Tendsto f atTop atTop â†’
    96â†’      âˆ€ a b : â„‌, 1 < a â†’ a < b â†’
    97â†’        âˆƒل¶  n in atTop,
    98â†’          a < F f n âˆ§ F f n < b := by
    99â†’  sorry
   100â†’

    18â†’/-!
    19â†’# Conjectures associated with A063880
    20â†’
    21â†’A063880 lists numbers $n$ such that $\sigma(n) = 2 \cdot \text{usigma}(n)$, where $\sigma(n)$ is the
    22â†’sum of all divisors and $\text{usigma}(n)$ is the sum of unitary divisors.
    23â†’
    24â†’Equivalently, these are numbers whose unitary and non-unitary divisors have equal sum.
    25â†’
    26â†’The conjectures state that all members satisfy $n \equiv 108 \pmod{216}$, and that all
    27â†’primitive terms (those whose proper divisors aren't in the sequence) are powerful numbers,
    28â†’with $108$ being the only primitive term.
    29â†’
    30â†’*References:* [A063880](https://oeis.org/A063880)
    31â†’-/
    32â†’
    33â†’open scoped ArithmeticFunction.sigma
    34â†’
    35â†’namespace OeisA63880
    36â†’
    37â†’/-- The set of unitary divisors of $n$: divisors $d$ such that $\gcd(d, n/d) = 1$. -/
    38â†’def unitaryDivisors (n : â„•) : Finset â„• :=
    39â†’  {d âˆˆ n.divisors | d.Coprime (n / d)}
    40â†’
    41â†’/-- The sum of unitary divisors of $n$, denoted $\text{usigma}(n)$. -/
    42â†’def usigma (n : â„•) : â„• :=
    43â†’  âˆ‘ d âˆˆ unitaryDivisors n, d
    44â†’
    45â†’/-- A number $n$ is in the sequence A063880 if $\sigma(n) = 2 \cdot \text{usigma}(n)$. -/
    46â†’def a (n : â„•) : Prop :=
    47â†’  0 < n âˆ§ دƒ 1 n = 2 * usigma n
    48â†’
    49â†’/-- The set of numbers in the sequence A063880. -/
    50â†’def A : Set â„• := {n | a n}
    51â†’
    52â†’/-- A term $n$ is primitive if no proper divisor of $n$ is in the sequence. -/
    53â†’abbrev isPrimitiveTerm (n : â„•) : Prop := A.IsPrimitive n
    54â†’
    55â†’/-- $108$ is in the sequence A063880. -/
    56â†’@[category test, AMS 11]
    57â†’theorem a_108 : a 108 := by
    58â†’  refine âں¨by norm_num, ?_âں©
    59â†’  decide
    60â†’
    61â†’/-- $540$ is in the sequence A063880. -/
    62â†’@[category test, AMS 11]
    63â†’theorem a_540 : a 540 := by
    64â†’  refine âں¨by norm_num, ?_âں©
    65â†’  decide
    66â†’
    67â†’/-- $756$ is in the sequence A063880. -/
    68â†’@[category test, AMS 11]
    69â†’theorem a_756 : a 756 := by
    70â†’  refine âں¨by norm_num, ?_âں©
    71â†’  decide
    72â†’
    73â†’/-- $108$ is a primitive term. -/
    74â†’@[category test, AMS 11]
    75â†’theorem isPrimitiveTerm_108 : isPrimitiveTerm 108 := by
    76â†’  rw [isPrimitiveTerm, Set.isPrimitive_iff]
    77â†’  refine âں¨a_108, ?_âں©
    78â†’  intro d hd
    79â†’  have âں¨hdvd, hltâں© := Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd
    80â†’  interval_cases d <;> simp_all [A, a] <;> decide
    81â†’
    82â†’/-- All members of the sequence satisfy $n \equiv 108 \pmod{216}$. -/
    83â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]
    84â†’theorem mod_216_of_a {n : â„•} (h : a n) : n % 216 = 108 := by
    85â†’  sorry
    86â†’
    87â†’/-- All primitive terms are powerful numbers. -/
    88â†’@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
    89â†’theorem powerful_of_isPrimitiveTerm {n : â„•} (h : isPrimitiveTerm n) : n.Powerful := by
    90â†’  sorry
    91â†’
    92â†’/-- $108$ is the only primitive term. -/
    93â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]
    94â†’theorem unique_primitive_108 {n : â„•} (h : isPrimitiveTerm n) : n = 108 := by
    95â†’  sorry
    96â†’
    97â†’/-- If $m$ is a primitive term and $s$ is squarefree with $\gcd(m, s) = 1$, then $m \cdot s$
    98â†’is in the sequence. -/
    99â†’@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
   100â†’theorem a_of_primitive_mul_squarefree (m s : â„•) (hm : isPrimitiveTerm m)
   101â†’    (hs : Squarefree s) (hcoprime : m.Coprime s) : a (m * s) := by
   102â†’  sorry
   103â†’
   104â†’/-- Non-primitive terms have the form $m \cdot s$ where $m$ is primitive and $s$ is
   105â†’squarefree with $\gcd(m, s) = 1$. -/
   106â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   107â†’theorem exists_primitive_of_a {n : â„•} (h : a n) :
   108â†’    âˆƒ m s, isPrimitiveTerm m âˆ§ Squarefree s âˆ§ m.Coprime s âˆ§ n = m * s := by
   109â†’  sorry
   110â†’
   111â†’end OeisA63880

    29â†’/--
    30â†’Is there an absolute constant $C > 0$ such that every integer $n$ with

    32â†’

    35â†’sum of proper divisors of $n$.

    37â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    38â†’theorem erdos_825 :
    39â†’    answer(True) â†” âˆƒ (C : â„‌) (_ : C > 0),
    40â†’      âˆ€ (n) (_ : دƒ 1 n > C * n),

    51â†’    (h : âˆ€ (n : â„•) (_ : دƒ 1 n > C * n),
    52â†’        âˆƒ s âٹ† n.properDivisors, n = s.sum id) :
    53â†’    2 < C := by

    27â†’[EGPS90] Erdإ‘s, P., Granville, A., Pomerance, C., and Spiro, C., _On the normal behavior of

    36â†’/-- The sum of proper divisors function $s(n) = \sum_{d \mid n,\, d < n} d$. -/

    35â†’/-- The number of divisors of $k$ that lie strictly between $n$ and $m$. -/
    36â†’def countDivisorsInOpenInterval (k n m : â„•) : â„• :=
    37â†’  ((Nat.divisors k).filter (fun d => n < d âˆ§ d < m)).card
    38â†’
    39â†’/-- The proportion of positive integers up to $N$ having exactly one divisor
    40â†’in the open interval $(n, m)$. This approximates $\delta_1(n, m)$. -/
    41â†’noncomputable def delta1Approx (n m N : â„•) : â„‌ :=
    42â†’  (((Finset.range (N + 1)).filter
    43â†’    (fun k => 0 < k âˆ§ countDivisorsInOpenInterval k n m = 1)).card : â„‌) / (N : â„‌)
    44â†’
    45â†’/-- The natural density $\delta_1(n, m)$ exists and equals $d$:
    46â†’the limit of $|\{k \leq N : k \text{ has exactly one divisor in } (n, m)\}| / N$ equals $d$. -/
    47â†’def HasDelta1Density (n m : â„•) (d : â„‌) : Prop :=
    48â†’  Filter.Tendsto (fun N => delta1Approx n m N) Filter.atTop (nhds d)
    49â†’
    50â†’/--
    51â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 692 [Er79e, Ob1] â€” DISPROVED
    52â†’
    53â†’Let $\delta_1(n, m)$ be the natural density of the set of positive integers with exactly
    54â†’one divisor in the open interval $(n, m)$. Erdإ‘s asked (1986, Oberwolfach) whether,
    55â†’for fixed $n$, $\delta_1(n, m)$ is unimodal as a function of $m$ for $m > n + 1$.
    56â†’
    57â†’Cambie [Ca25] disproved this by showing it fails even for $n = 3$:
    58â†’$$\delta_1(3, 6) = 0.35, \quad \delta_1(3, 7) \approx 0.33, \quad \delta_1(3, 8) \approx 0.3619,$$
    59â†’exhibiting a "valley" at $m = 7$ that contradicts unimodality.
    60â†’Furthermore, for fixed $n$, the sequence $\delta_1(n, m)$ has superpolynomially many
    61â†’local maxima in $m$.
    62â†’
    63â†’The RHS formalizes unimodality: for every $n$ and $m_1 < m_2 < m_3$ (all $> n + 1$),
    64â†’the density at $m_2$ is not a strict local minimum (i.e., $d_1 \le d_2$ or $d_3 \le d_2$).
    65â†’The answer is `False` because Cambie exhibited a counterexample.
    66â†’-/
    67â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    68â†’theorem erdos_692 : answer(False) â†”
    69â†’    âˆ€ n : â„•, âˆ€ mâ‚پ mâ‚‚ mâ‚ƒ : â„•,
    70â†’      n + 1 < mâ‚پ â†’ mâ‚پ < mâ‚‚ â†’ mâ‚‚ < mâ‚ƒ â†’
    71â†’      âˆ€ dâ‚پ dâ‚‚ dâ‚ƒ : â„‌,
    72â†’        HasDelta1Density n mâ‚پ dâ‚پ â†’
    73â†’        HasDelta1Density n mâ‚‚ dâ‚‚ â†’
    74â†’        HasDelta1Density n mâ‚ƒ dâ‚ƒ â†’
    75â†’        dâ‚پ â‰¤ dâ‚‚ âˆ¨ dâ‚ƒ â‰¤ dâ‚‚ := by

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 824
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/824](https://www.erdosproblems.com/824)
    23â†’
    24â†’Let $h(x)$ count the number of integers $1 \le a < b < x$ such that $(a,b) = 1$ and
    25â†’$\sigma(a) = \sigma(b)$, where $\sigma$ is the sum of divisors function.
    26â†’
    27â†’Is it true that $h(x) > x^{2-o(1)}$?

    31â†’Pomerance [PoPo16].

    33â†’[Er59c] Erdإ‘s, P., _Remarks on number theory II. Some problems on the دƒ function_.
    34â†’Acta Arith. 5 (1959), 171-177.
    35â†’
    36â†’[Er74b] Erdإ‘s, P., _On abundant-like numbers_. Canad. Math. Bull. 17 (1974), 599-602.

    46â†’/-- The sum of divisors function $\sigma(n) = \sum_{d \mid n} d$. -/

    53â†’    (fun p => 0 < p.1 âˆ§ p.1 < p.2 âˆ§ Nat.Coprime p.1 p.2 âˆ§

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 350
    21â†’
    22â†’*References:*
    23â†’- [erdosproblems.com/350](https://www.erdosproblems.com/350)
    24â†’- [BeEr74] Benkoski, S. J. and Erdإ‘s, P., On weird and pseudoperfect numbers. Math. Comp. (1974),
    25â†’  617-623.
    26â†’- [HSS77] Hanson, F. and Steele, J. M. and Stenger, F., Distinct sums over subsets. Proc. Amer.
    27â†’  Math. Soc. (1977), 179-180.

    30â†’namespace Erdos350
    31â†’
    32â†’/-- The predicate that all (finite) subsets of `A` have distinct sums. -/
    33â†’def DistinctSubsetSums {M : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M] (A : Set M) : Prop :=
    34â†’  Set.Pairwise {X : Finset M | â†‘X âٹ† A} fun X Y => X.sum id â‰  Y.sum id
    35â†’
    36â†’/-- The predicate that all (finite) subsets of `A` have distinct sums, decidable version -/
    37â†’def DecidableDistinctSubsetSums {M : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M] [DecidableEq M] (A : Finset M) : Prop :=
    38â†’  âˆ€ X âٹ† A, âˆ€ Y âٹ† A, X â‰  Y â†’ X.sum id â‰  Y.sum id
    39â†’
    40â†’@[category test, AMS 5 11]
    41â†’theorem decidableDistinctSubsetSums_1_2 : DecidableDistinctSubsetSums {1, 2} := by
    42â†’  rw [DecidableDistinctSubsetSums] ; decide
    43â†’
    44â†’@[category test, AMS 5 11]
    45â†’theorem distinctSubsetSums_1_2 : DistinctSubsetSums ({1, 2} : Set â„•) := by
    46â†’  simp only [DistinctSubsetSums, Set.Pairwise, Set.mem_setOf_eq, ne_eq, id_eq]
    47â†’  intro x hx y hy hxy
    48â†’  -- FIXME: Why is `norm_cast` useless here?
    49â†’  simp_rw [â†گ Finset.coe_singleton, â†گ Finset.coe_insert, Finset.coe_subset, â†گFinset.mem_powerset] at *
    50â†’  fin_cases hx <;> fin_cases hy <;> simp_all
    51â†’
    52â†’/-- Small sanity check: the two predicates are saying the same thing. -/
    53â†’@[category API, AMS 5 11]
    54â†’theorem DistinctSubsetSums_iff_DecidableDistinctSubsetSums
    55â†’    {M : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M] [DecidableEq M] (A : Finset M) :
    56â†’    DistinctSubsetSums (A : Set M) â†” DecidableDistinctSubsetSums A := by
    57â†’  rw [DistinctSubsetSums, DecidableDistinctSubsetSums, Set.Pairwise] ; simp_all
    58â†’
    59â†’/--
    60â†’If `A âٹ‚ â„•` is a finite set of integers all of whose subset sums are distinct then `âˆ‘ n âˆˆ A, 1/n < 2`.
    61â†’Proved by Ryavec.
    62â†’
    63â†’This was proved by Ryavec, who did not appear to ever publish the proof. Ryavec's proof is
    64â†’reproduced in [BeEr74]. More generally, Ryavec's proof delivers that
    65â†’$\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}\leq 2-2^{1-\lvert A\rvert},$ with equality if and only if
    66â†’$A=\{1,2,\ldots,2^k\}$.
    67â†’
    68â†’This was formalized in Lean by Alexeev using Aristotle.
    69â†’-/
    70â†’@[category research formally solved using lean4 at
    71â†’"https://github.com/plby/lean-proofs/blob/main/src/v4.24.0/ErdosProblems/Erdos350.lean", AMS 5 11]
    72â†’theorem erdos_350 (A : Finset â„•) (hA : DecidableDistinctSubsetSums A) :
    73â†’    âˆ‘ n âˆˆ A, (1 / n : â„‌) < 2 := by
    74â†’  sorry
    75â†’
    76â†’/--
    77â†’If `A âٹ‚ â„•` is a finite set of integers all of whose subset sums are distinct then `âˆ‘ n âˆˆ A, 1/n^s < 1/(1 - 2^(-s))`, for any `s > 0`.
    78â†’Proved by Hanson, Steele, and Stenger [HSS77].
    79â†’
    80â†’We exlude here the case `s = 0`, because in the informal formulation then the right hand side is to be interpreted as `âˆ‍`, while the left hand side counts the elements in `A`.
    81â†’-/
    82â†’@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    83â†’theorem erdos_350.variants.strengthening (A : Finset â„•) (hA : DecidableDistinctSubsetSums A)
    84â†’    (s : â„‌) (hs : 0 < s) : âˆ‘ n âˆˆ A, (1 / n : â„‌)^s < 1 / (1 - 2^(-s)) := by
    85â†’  sorry

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 673
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/673](https://www.erdosproblems.com/673)
    23â†’
    24â†’Let $1 = d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)} = n$ be the divisors of $n$ and
    25â†’$$G(n) = \sum_{1 \leq i < \tau(n)} d_i / d_{i+1}.$$
    26â†’
    27â†’Is it true that $G(n) \to \infty$ for almost all $n$? Can one prove an asymptotic
    28â†’formula for $\sum_{n \leq X} G(n)$?
    29â†’
    30â†’Erdإ‘s writes it is 'easy' to prove $(1/X) \sum_{n \leq X} G(n) \to \infty$.
    31â†’
    32â†’Terence Tao observed that for any divisor $m \mid n$,
    33â†’$\tau(n/m) / m \leq G(n) \leq \tau(n)$,
    34â†’and hence for example $\tau(n)/4 \leq G(n) \leq \tau(n)$ for even $n$. It follows easily
    35â†’that $G(n)$ grows on average and tends to infinity for almost all $n$.
    36â†’
    37â†’Erdإ‘s and Tenenbaum proved that $G(n)/\tau(n)$ has a continuous distribution
    38â†’function.
    39â†’
    40â†’[Er79e] Erdإ‘s, P., _Some unconventional problems in number theory_ (1979).
    41â†’
    42â†’[Er82e] Erdإ‘s, P., _Some new problems and results in number theory_ (1982).
    43â†’-/
    44â†’
    45â†’open Filter
    46â†’
    47â†’namespace Erdos673
    48â†’
    49â†’/-- The sorted list of divisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/
    50â†’def sortedDivisors (n : â„•) : List â„• :=
    51â†’  (Nat.divisors n).sort (آ· â‰¤ آ·)
    52â†’
    53â†’/-- $G(n) = \sum_{1 \leq i < \tau(n)} d_i / d_{i+1}$, where $d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)}$ are the
    54â†’divisors of $n$ in increasing order. This is the sum of ratios of consecutive
    55â†’divisors. -/
    56â†’noncomputable def erdosG (n : â„•) : â„‌ :=
    57â†’  let divs := sortedDivisors n
    58â†’  ((divs.zip divs.tail).map (fun p => (p.1 : â„‌) / (p.2 : â„‌))).sum
    59â†’
    60â†’/-- The natural density of a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is zero. -/
    61â†’def HasNaturalDensityZero (A : Set â„•) [DecidablePred (آ· âˆˆ A)] : Prop :=
    62â†’  Tendsto
    63â†’    (fun N : â„• => (((Finset.range N).filter (آ· âˆˆ A)).card : â„‌) / (N : â„‌))
    64â†’    atTop (nhds 0)
    65â†’
    66â†’/--
    67â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 673, Part 1 [Er79e, Er82e]:
    68â†’
    69â†’$G(n) \to \infty$ for almost all $n$, i.e., for every $M$, the set of $n$ with $G(n) \leq M$
    70â†’has natural density zero.
    71â†’-/
    72â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    73â†’theorem erdos_673 :
    74â†’    answer(True) â†” âˆ€ M : â„‌, HasNaturalDensityZero {n : â„• | erdosG n â‰¤ M} := by
    75â†’  sorry
    76â†’
    77â†’/--
    78â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 673, Part 2 [Er79e, Er82e]:
    79â†’
    80â†’$(1/X) \cdot \sum_{n \leq X} G(n) \to \infty$ as $X \to \infty$.
    81â†’-/
    82â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    83â†’theorem erdos_673.variants.average :
    84â†’    Tendsto
    85â†’      (fun X : â„• => (Finset.sum (Finset.range X) erdosG) / (X : â„‌))
    86â†’      atTop atTop := by
    87â†’  sorry
    88â†’
    89â†’end Erdos673

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 381
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/381](https://www.erdosproblems.com/381)
    23â†’
    24â†’Is it true that $Q(x) \gg_k (\log x)^k$ for every $k \geq 1$, where $Q(x)$
    25â†’counts the number of highly composite numbers in $[1, x]$? Disproved by
    26â†’Nicolas [Ni71].
    27â†’
    28â†’[Er44] Erdإ‘s, P., _On highly composite numbers_,
    29â†’J. London Math. Soc. 19 (1944), 130â€“133.
    30â†’
    31â†’[Ni71] Nicolas, J.-L., _Rأ©partition des nombres hautement composأ©s
    32â†’de Ramanujan_, Canad. J. Math. 23 (1971), 116â€“130.
    33â†’-/
    34â†’
    35â†’open Classical Finset Real
    36â†’
    37â†’namespace Erdos381
    38â†’
    39â†’/-- A natural number $n$ is highly composite if every smaller positive natural
    40â†’number has strictly fewer divisors. -/
    41â†’def IsHighlyComposite (n : â„•) : Prop :=
    42â†’  0 < n âˆ§ âˆ€ m : â„•, 0 < m â†’ m < n â†’ (Nat.divisors m).card < (Nat.divisors n).card
    43â†’
    44â†’/-- `highlyCompositeCount x` counts the number of highly composite numbers in $[1, x]$. -/
    45â†’noncomputable def highlyCompositeCount (x : â„•) : â„• :=
    46â†’  ((Finset.range x).filter (fun n => IsHighlyComposite (n + 1))).card
    47â†’
    48â†’/--
    49â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 381 (Disproved) [Er44]:
    50â†’
    51â†’A number $n$ is highly composite if $\tau(m) < \tau(n)$ for all $m < n$, where $\tau(m)$
    52â†’counts the number of divisors of $m$. Let $Q(x)$ count the number of highly
    53â†’composite numbers in $[1, x]$.
    54â†’
    55â†’Erdإ‘s asked whether $Q(x) \gg_k (\log x)^k$ for every $k \geq 1$.
    56â†’
    57â†’Erdإ‘s [Er44] proved $Q(x) \gg (\log x)^{1+c}$ for some constant $c > 0$.
    58â†’
    59â†’The answer is no: Nicolas [Ni71] showed that $Q(x)$ does not grow faster
    60â†’than a fixed power of $\log x$.
    61â†’-/
    62â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    63â†’theorem erdos_381 :
    64â†’    answer(False) â†”
    65â†’      âˆ€ (k : â„•), 1 â‰¤ k â†’ âˆƒ c : â„‌, 0 < c âˆ§
    66â†’        âˆƒ Nâ‚€ : â„•, âˆ€ x : â„•, Nâ‚€ â‰¤ x â†’
    67â†’          c * (Real.log (x : â„‌)) ^ k â‰¤ (highlyCompositeCount x : â„‌) := by
    68â†’  sorry

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 18
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/18](https://www.erdosproblems.com/18)
    23â†’
    24â†’We call $m$ *practical* if every integer $1 \leq n < m$ is the sum of distinct divisors
    25â†’of $m$. If $m$ is practical, let $h(m)$ be the least $k$ such that every $1 \leq n < m$
    26â†’is the sum of at most $k$ distinct divisors of $m$.
    27â†’
    28â†’Three questions:
    29â†’1. Are there infinitely many practical $m$ such that $h(m) < (\log \log m)^{O(1)}$?
    30â†’2. Is it true that $h(n!) < n^{o(1)}$?
    31â†’3. Or perhaps even $h(n!) < (\log n)^{O(1)}$?
    32â†’
    33â†’Known: Erdإ‘s showed $h(n!) < n$. Vose [Vo85] proved infinitely many practical $m$ with
    34â†’$h(m) \ll (\log m)^{1/2}$. Erdإ‘s offered \$250 for a proof or disproof of whether
    35â†’$h(n!) < (\log n)^{O(1)}$ [Er81h].
    36â†’
    37â†’## References

    48â†’/-- $m$ is practical if every integer $1 \leq n < m$ can be represented as a sum
    49â†’    of distinct divisors of $m$. -/

    52â†’    âˆƒ S : Finset â„•, S âٹ† Nat.divisors m âˆ§ S.sum id = n

    56â†’    divisors of $m$. -/

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 122
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/122](https://www.erdosproblems.com/122)
    23â†’
    24â†’For which arithmetic functions $f$ is it true that the count of $n$ with $n + f(n)$ in a
    25â†’short interval $(x, x + F(x))$, divided by $F(x)$, is unbounded? Erdإ‘sâ€“Pomeranceâ€“Sأ،rkأ¶zy
    26â†’proved this for $f = \tau$ (divisor count) and $f = \omega$ (distinct prime factor count).
    27â†’
    28â†’[Er97] Erdإ‘s, P., *Some of my new and almost new problems and results in combinatorial
    29â†’number theory* (1997).
    30â†’
    31â†’[Er97e] Erdإ‘s, P., *Some problems and results on combinatorial number theory* (1997).
    32â†’
    33â†’[EPS97] Erdإ‘s, P., Pomerance, C., and Sأ،rkأ¶zy, A., *On locally repeated values of certain
    34â†’arithmetic functions, III* (1997).
    35â†’-/
    36â†’
    37â†’open scoped Classical
    38â†’
    39â†’open Filter
    40â†’
    41â†’namespace Erdos122
    42â†’
    43â†’/-- The count of natural numbers $n$ such that $n + f(n)$ falls in the open real
    44â†’interval $(x, x + F(x))$. Since $n \le n + f(n) < x + F(x)$ requires $n < x + F(x)$,
    45â†’it suffices to search $n$ in the range $[0, x + \lceil F(x) \rceil]$. -/
    46â†’noncomputable def shiftCount (f : â„• â†’ â„•) (F : â„• â†’ â„‌) (x : â„•) : â„• :=
    47â†’  ((Finset.range (x + âŒˆF xâŒ‰â‚ٹ + 1)).filter
    48â†’    (fun (n : â„•) => (x : â„‌) < (n : â„‌) + (f n : â„‌) âˆ§
    49â†’              (n : â„‌) + (f n : â„‌) < (x : â„‌) + F x)).card
    50â†’
    51â†’/-- The natural density of a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is zero: the proportion of elements
    52â†’in $\{0, \ldots, N-1\}$ belonging to $A$ tends to $0$ as $N \to \infty$. -/
    53â†’def HasNaturalDensityZero (A : Set â„•) : Prop :=
    54â†’  Tendsto
    55â†’    (fun N : â„• => ((Finset.range N).filter (آ· âˆˆ A)).card / (N : â„‌))
    56â†’    atTop (nhds 0)
    57â†’
    58â†’/-- $f(n)/F(n) \to 0$ for almost all $n$ in the natural-density sense:
    59â†’for every $\varepsilon > 0$, the set $\{n : f(n)/F(n) \ge \varepsilon\}$ has natural
    60â†’density zero. -/
    61â†’def AlmostAllRatioVanishes (f : â„• â†’ â„•) (F : â„• â†’ â„‌) : Prop :=
    62â†’  âˆ€ خµ : â„‌, 0 < خµ â†’ HasNaturalDensityZero {n : â„• | خµ â‰¤ (f n : â„‌) / F n}
    63â†’
    64â†’/-- The Erdإ‘s-122 property of $f$: for any positive $F$ with $f(n)/F(n) \to 0$
    65â†’in natural density, the ratio $\#\{n \in \mathbb{N} : n+f(n) \in (x, x+F(x))\} / F(x)$
    66â†’is unbounded as $x \to \infty$ (equivalently, its limsup equals $+\infty$). -/
    67â†’def HasErdos122Property (f : â„• â†’ â„•) : Prop :=
    68â†’  âˆ€ F : â„• â†’ â„‌, (âˆ€ n, 0 < F n) â†’ AlmostAllRatioVanishes f F â†’
    69â†’    âˆ€ C : â„‌, âˆƒل¶  x : â„• in atTop, C < (shiftCount f F x : â„‌) / F x
    70â†’
    71â†’/--
    72â†’Erdإ‘s Problem #122 [Er97, Er97e, EPS97]
    73â†’
    74â†’For which number-theoretic functions $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ is it true that for any
    75â†’positive function $F : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfying $f(n)/F(n) \to 0$ for almost all $n$
    76â†’(in the natural density sense), there are infinitely many $x$ for which
    77â†’$$\#\{n \in \mathbb{N} : n + f(n) \in (x, x + F(x))\} / F(x) \to \infty?$$
    78â†’
    79â†’Erdإ‘s, Pomerance, and Sأ،rkأ¶zy [EPS97] proved that this holds when:
    80â†’- $f(n) = \tau(n)$ = number of divisors of $n$, and
    81â†’- $f(n) = \omega(n)$ = number of distinct prime divisors of $n$.
    82â†’
    83â†’Erdإ‘s believed the property to be FALSE for:
    84â†’- $f(n) = \varphi(n)$ (Euler's totient function), and
    85â†’- $f(n) = \sigma(n)$ (sum of divisors).
    86â†’
    87â†’The full characterization of which $f$ satisfy `HasErdos122Property` remains open.
    88â†’The theorem below records the two proved instances.
    89â†’-/
    90â†’@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    91â†’theorem erdos_122 :
    92â†’    HasErdos122Property (fun n => (Nat.divisors n).card) âˆ§
    93â†’    HasErdos122Property (fun n => (Nat.primeFactorsList n).toFinset.card) := by
    94â†’  sorry
    95â†’
    96â†’end Erdos122

    23â†’
    24â†’Call a number $k$-perfect if $\sigma(n) = kn$, where $\sigma(n)$ is the sum of the divisors
    25â†’of $n$. Must $k = o(\log \log n)$?
    26â†’
    27â†’A question of Erdإ‘s, as reported in problem B2 of Guy's collection.
    28â†’
    29â†’[Gu04] Guy, R., *Unsolved Problems in Number Theory*, 3rd edition, Springer, 2004.

    31â†’
    32â†’open Finset Real

    35â†’
    36â†’/-- The sum-of-divisors function $\sigma(n) = \sum_{d \mid n} d$. -/

    40â†’/-- A natural number $n$ is $k$-perfect if $n \geq 1$ and $\sigma(n) = k \cdot n$. -/

    44â†’/--
    45â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 1053 [Gu04]:
    46â†’
    47â†’If $n$ is a $k$-perfect number ($\sigma(n) = kn$), must $k = o(\log \log n)$ as $n \to \infty$
    48â†’among multiply perfect numbers?
    49â†’
    50â†’Equivalently: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, the set of multiply perfect numbers $n$

    52â†’-/
    53â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]

    55â†’    âˆ€ خµ : â„‌, خµ > 0 â†’
    56â†’    Set.Finite {n : â„• | âˆƒ k : â„•, IsMultiplyPerfect n k âˆ§
    57â†’      (k : â„‌) â‰¥ خµ * Real.log (Real.log (n : â„‌))} := by

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 1099
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1099](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1099)
    23â†’
    24â†’Let $1 = d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)} = n$ be the divisors of $n$, and for $\alpha > 1$ let
    25â†’$$h_\alpha(n) = \sum_i \left(\frac{d_{i+1}}{d_i} - 1\right)^\alpha.$$
    26â†’
    27â†’Is it true that $\liminf_{n \to \infty} h_\alpha(n) \ll_\alpha 1$?
    28â†’
    29â†’Erdإ‘s [Er81h] remarks that $n!$ or $\mathrm{lcm}\{1, \ldots, n\}$ would be good candidates.
    30â†’The $\liminf$ is trivially $\geq 1$ (considering the term $i = 1$).
    31â†’
    32â†’Proved by Vose [Vo84] who constructed a specific sequence achieving bounded $h_\alpha(n)$.
    33â†’It remains open whether $n!$ or $\mathrm{lcm}\{1, \ldots, n\}$ satisfy this property.
    34â†’
    35â†’This resembles the function $G(n) = \sum_i d_i / d_{i+1}$ considered in problem \#673.
    36â†’
    37â†’[Er81h] Erdإ‘s, P., _Some problems and results in number theory_.
    38â†’
    39â†’[Vo84] Vose, M. D., _Integers with consecutive divisors in small ratio_, J. Number Theory (1984).
    40â†’-/
    41â†’
    42â†’open Classical Finset Real
    43â†’
    44â†’namespace Erdos1099
    45â†’
    46â†’/-- The sorted list of divisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/
    47â†’def sortedDivisors (n : â„•) : List â„• :=
    48â†’  (Nat.divisors n).sort (آ· â‰¤ آ·)
    49â†’
    50â†’/-- $h_\alpha(n) = \sum_i (d_{i+1}/d_i - 1)^\alpha$ where $d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)}$ are the
    51â†’divisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/
    52â†’noncomputable def hAlpha (خ± : â„‌) (n : â„•) : â„‌ :=
    53â†’  let ds := sortedDivisors n
    54â†’  ((ds.zip ds.tail).map (fun p => ((p.2 : â„‌) / (p.1 : â„‌) - 1) ^ خ±)).sum
    55â†’
    56â†’/--
    57â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 1099 (Proved by Vose [Vo84]):
    58â†’
    59â†’For every $\alpha > 1$, there exists a constant $C$ (depending on $\alpha$) such that
    60â†’$h_\alpha(n) \leq C$ for infinitely many $n$, i.e., $\liminf_{n \to \infty} h_\alpha(n)$
    61â†’is finite.
    62â†’
    63â†’Formally: for every $\alpha > 1$, there exists $C$ such that for every $N$, there
    64â†’exists $n \geq N$ with $h_\alpha(n) \leq C$.

    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 1100
    21â†’

    23â†’
    24â†’If $1 = d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)} = n$ are the divisors of $n$, let $\tau^\perp(n)$ count the
    25â†’number of $i$ for which $\gcd(d_i, d_{i+1}) = 1$.
    26â†’
    27â†’Part 1: Is it true that $\tau^\perp(n)/\omega(n) \to \infty$ for almost all $n$?
    28â†’
    29â†’Part 2: Is it true that $\tau^\perp(n) < \exp((\log n)^{o(1)})$ for all $n$?
    30â†’Equivalently, for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and sufficiently large $n$,
    31â†’$\tau^\perp(n) < \exp((\log n)^\varepsilon)$.
    32â†’
    33â†’Part 3: Let $g(k) = \max$ over squarefree $n$ with $\omega(n) = k$ of $\tau^\perp(n)$.
    34â†’Determine the growth of $g(k)$. Erdإ‘s and Simonovits proved
    35â†’$(\sqrt{2} + o(1))^k < g(k) < (2 - c)^k$ for some constant $c > 0$.
    36â†’-/
    37â†’
    38â†’open Finset Real
    39â†’
    40â†’namespace Erdos1100
    41â†’
    42â†’/-- The sorted list of divisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/

    44â†’  (Nat.divisors n).sort (آ· â‰¤ آ·)
    45â†’
    46â†’/-- $\tau^\perp(n)$: the number of indices $i$ such that $\gcd(d_i, d_{i+1}) = 1$,
    47â†’where $d_1 < \cdots < d_{\tau(n)}$ are the divisors of $n$ in increasing order. -/
    48â†’def tauPerp (n : â„•) : â„• :=
    49â†’  let ds := sortedDivisors n
    50â†’  ((ds.zip ds.tail).filter (fun p => Nat.gcd p.1 p.2 == 1)).length
    51â†’
    52â†’/--
    53â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 1100, Part 1:
    54â†’Is it true that $\tau^\perp(n)/\omega(n) \to \infty$ for almost all $n$? That is, for every
    55â†’bound $M$, the natural density of $\{n : \tau^\perp(n) \le M \cdot \omega(n)\}$ is zero.
    56â†’-/
    57â†’@[category research open, AMS 11]
    58â†’theorem erdos_1100 :
    59â†’    answer(sorry) â†”
    60â†’      âˆ€ M : â„•, âˆ€ خµ : â„‌, خµ > 0 â†’
    61â†’        âˆƒ Nâ‚€ : â„•, âˆ€ N : â„•, N â‰¥ Nâ‚€ â†’
    62â†’          (((Finset.range N).filter (fun n =>
    63â†’            tauPerp n â‰¤ M * n.primeFactors.card)).card : â„‌) / (N : â„‌) < خµ := by
    64â†’  sorry
    65â†’
    66â†’/--
    67â†’Erdإ‘s Problem 1100, Part 2:
    68â†’Is it true that for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $n$,
    69â†’$\tau^\perp(n) < \exp((\log n)^\varepsilon)$?
    70â†’This formalizes the conjecture that $\tau^\perp(n) < \exp((\log n)^{o(1)})$.
